Verbatim Snippet: For a Category A activity, the relief that a court may grant against a service provider is limited to one or more of the following orders: (a) an order requiring the service provider to take reasonable steps to disable access to an online location outside Australia; (b) an order requiring the service provider to terminate a specified account.
Mapping Rationale: This s. 116AG (Part V, Division 2AA) caps the remedies a court may grant against a carriage/service provider that meets the safe-harbour conditions in s. 116AH — Australia's copyright intermediary safe harbour. Maps to P8-I1.
Notes: Safe-harbour conditions in s.116AH (repeat-infringer policy, notice-and-takedown). 2018 amendment extended cover beyond CSPs to libraries, educational and cultural institutions.

Verbatim Snippet: An APP entity must not do an act, or engage in a practice, that breaches an Australian Privacy Principle.
Mapping Rationale: This s. 15 imposes a general duty to comply with all 13 APPs. Maps to 7.1 (comprehensive framework).
Notes: Core enforcement anchor for the 13 APPs (s.14). Breach triggers civil penalties under s.13G.

Verbatim Snippet: The period for which a service provider must keep ... under section 187A is: (a) ... ending 2 years after the closure of the account
Mapping Rationale: This s. 187C(1) fixes a mandatory 2-year minimum retention period for telecommunications data. Maps to 7.3 (minimum retention period).
Notes: Companion s.187AA(1) table defines the dataset categories subject to the 2-year rule.

Verbatim Snippet: (1) If the Director-General requests the Attorney-General to do so ... the Attorney-General may issue a warrant in accordance with this section.
Mapping Rationale: This s. 25A empowers the Attorney-General to authorise computer-access warrants. Maps to 7.5 (access without judicial sign-off).
Notes: DB also cites the Data Availability and Transparency Act 2022 (s.104) and TOLA 2018 notice powers.

Verbatim Snippet: The entity must: (a) if it is practicable ... take such steps as are reasonable in the circumstances to notify the contents of the statement
Mapping Rationale: This s. 26WL(2) requires mandatory notification following an eligible data breach. Maps to 7.1 (comprehensive framework).
Notes: NEW because the DB row only referenced the Act generally and the OAIC NDB guidance page.

Verbatim Snippet: ...the purpose of which is to do the following for each of those assets: (i) identify each hazard where there is a material risk ... (ii) minimise or eliminate any material risk of such a hazard occurring
Mapping Rationale: This s. 30AH(1) mandates a critical infrastructure risk management program covering cyber hazards. Maps to 7.2 (sector-specific cybersecurity).
Notes: Opening words of s.30AH(1)(a) not fully captured. Criminal Code Act 1995 Part 10.7 also cited in DB.

Law Name: Telecommunications and Other Legislation Amendment (Assistance and Access) Act 2018
Verbatim Snippet: The Director-General of Security or the chief officer of an interception agency may give a designated communications provider a notice, to be known as a technical assistance notice
Mapping Rationale: This s. 317L(1) empowers ASIO to compel technical assistance without judicial authorisation. Maps to 7.5 (executive compulsion power).
Notes: One of three related notice types (TAR s.317G

Verbatim Snippet: A person is guilty of an offence if: (a) the person causes any unauthorised access to, or modification of, restricted data; and (b) the person intends to cause the access or modification
Mapping Rationale: This s. 478.1 criminalises unauthorised access to restricted computer data. Maps to 7.2 (criminal-law limb).
Notes: Snippet truncated mid-clause in search result. Verify full text against legislation.gov.au.

Verbatim Snippet: must not: ... (b) process or handle the information relating to the records outside Australia
Mapping Rationale: This s. 77(1)(b) prohibits processing or handling My Health Record data outside Australia. Maps to 6.1 (ban and local processing).
Notes: Text confirmed via AustLII mirror of s.77; direct legislation.gov.au fetch blocked bot detection.

Verbatim Snippet: The System Operator ... must not: (a) hold the records, or take the records, outside Australia
Mapping Rationale: This s. 77(1)(a) prohibits holding or storing My Health Record data outside Australia. Maps to 6.2 (local storage requirements).
Notes: Single provision split across two RDTII indicators per methodology. Sector-specific (health).

Verbatim Snippet: If an APP entity holds personal information, the entity must take such steps as are reasonable in the circumstances to protect the information: (a) from misuse, interference and loss; and (b) from unauthorised access, modification or disclosure.
Mapping Rationale: This cl. 11.1 imposes a general data-security obligation on all APP entities. Maps to 7.2 (horizontal security duty).
Notes: Privacy Act's contribution to Australia's dispersed cybersecurity patchwork.

Verbatim Snippet: Before an APP entity discloses personal information about an individual to a person (the overseas recipient) ... the entity must take such steps as are reasonable in the circumstances to ensure that the overseas recipient does not breach the Australian Privacy Principles
Mapping Rationale: This cl. 8.1 requires reasonable steps before disclosing personal data overseas. Maps to 6.4 (conditional flow regimes).
Notes: Clause 8.1(b) omitted from snippet - direct fetch blocked. Exceptions in cl. 8.2 further condition the flow regime.

Verbatim Snippet: The Commissioner may give the provider of the service a written notice, to be known as a removal notice, requiring the provider to take all reasonable steps to ensure the removal of the material from the service.
Mapping Rationale: This s. 109(1) empowers the eSafety Commissioner to compel a social media / electronic / internet service to remove class 1 material — an intermediary take-down obligation with no general safe harbour. Maps to P8-I2 (lack of safe harbour for other illegal activities).
Notes: Removal notice for class 1 material (s.106). Verbatim per Federal Register / AustLII s.109. Non-compliance carries civil penalties; tested in eSafety Commissioner v X Corp (2024).

Verbatim Snippet: (1) A foreign company must not carry on business in this jurisdiction unless: (a) it is registered under this Division; or (b) it has applied to be so registered
Mapping Rationale: This s.601CD(1) prohibits a foreign company from carrying on business in Australia without ASIC registration. Maps to 12.8 (local presence).
Notes: Verified via AustLII.

Verbatim Snippet: The owner of a copyright may apply to the Federal Court of Australia to grant an injunction that requires a carriage service provider to take such steps as the Court considers reasonable to disable access to an online location outside Australia
Mapping Rationale: This s.115A(1) authorises court-ordered site-blocking of infringing overseas locations. Maps to 9.1.
Notes: 2018 Amendment broadened test to primary purpose or primary effect.

Law Name: Criminal Code Amendment (Sharing of Abhorrent Violent Material) Act 2019
Verbatim Snippet: A person commits an offence if: (a) the person provides a hosting service; and (b) material is hosted on the hosting service; and (c) the material is abhorrent violent material; and (d) the person does not expeditiously cease hosting the material.
Mapping Rationale: This s.474.34(5) imposes criminal liability on hosting services that fail to remove abhorrent violent material. Maps to 8.2.
Notes: Fetched and confirmed directly against the primary Federal Register text.

Verbatim Snippet: Notwithstanding anything contained in any law for the time being in force but subject to the provisions of sub-sections (2) and (3), an intermediary shall not be liable for any third party information, data, or communication link made available or hosted by him.
Mapping Rationale: This s. 79(1) establishes a conditional safe harbour shielding intermediaries from liability for third-party content, including copyright material, provided the due-diligence conditions in s. 79(2)-(3) are met. Maps to P8-I1 as the presence-of-safe-harbour evidence for copyright infringements.
Notes: Conditional safe harbour: exemption lost under s.79(3) on actual knowledge / failure to expeditiously take down. Due-diligence obligations prescribed by the IT (Intermediary Guidelines) Rules 2021.

Verbatim Snippet: Notwithstanding anything contained in any law for the time being in force but subject to the provisions of sub-sections (2) and (3), an intermediary shall not be liable for any third party information, data, or communication link made available or hosted by him.
Mapping Rationale: The same s. 79(1) safe harbour is horizontal — it covers third-party information generally, not only copyright — so it also supplies the presence-of-safe-harbour evidence for other unlawful activities. Maps to P8-I2.
Notes: Same provision cited for P8-I1 and P8-I2 because s.79 is a general (non-subject-matter-specific) intermediary immunity. Shrreya Singhal v. Union of India read down s.79(3)(b) to require a court/government order.

Law Name: Information Technology (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules, 2021
Verbatim Snippet: A significant social media intermediary providing services primarily in the nature of messaging shall enable the identification of the first originator of the information on its computer resource as may be required by a judicial order passed by a competent court or an order passed under section 69 by the Competent Authority.
Mapping Rationale: This Rule 4(2) imposes a traceability / first-originator identification obligation on significant social media messaging intermediaries. Maps to P8-I4 as an active monitoring/traceability requirement conditioning the safe harbour.
Notes: Traceability duty is triggered only for the most serious offences (imprisonment >= 5 years) and does not require disclosure of message content. Challenged as undermining end-to-end encryption.

Law Name: The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023
Mapping Rationale: Section 16 empowers the Central Government to notify specific countries or territories for personal data transfer, establishing a conditional flow regime based on government assessment and prescribed terms. This directly addresses the 'restriction to notified countries' mentioned in the seed.
Notes: Verbatim text for Section 16 could not be retrieved from the provided source snippet [S1], which only includes up to Section 3. However, the existence and content of Section 16 regarding cross-border data transfers are confirmed from the full Act (No. 22 of 2023).

Law Name: The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023
Verbatim Snippet: The Central Government may, after an assessment of such factors as it may consider necessary, notify certain countries or territories outside India to which a Data Fiduciary may transfer personal data, subject to such terms and conditions as may be prescribed.
Mapping Rationale: This section grants the Central Government the power to notify countries for cross-border data transfers, subject to conditions, establishing a conditional flow regime. Maps to P6-I4.
Notes: This provision allows for the regulation of cross-border data transfers based on government assessment.

Law Name: The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023
Verbatim Snippet: This Act may be called the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023.
Mapping Rationale: This section establishes the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, which provides a comprehensive legal framework for data protection in India. Maps to P7-I1 by demonstrating the existence of such a framework.
Notes: The Act received Presidential assent on 11th August 2023. Commencement for different provisions may be appointed by notification.

Law Name: The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023
Verbatim Snippet: A person may process the personal data of a Data Principal only in accordance with the provisions of this Act and for a lawful purpose,— (a) for which the Data Principal has given her consent; or (b) for certain legitimate uses.
Mapping Rationale: This section outlines the fundamental obligation of a Data Fiduciary to process personal data only with the Data Principal's consent or for legitimate uses, forming a core part of the data protection framework. Maps to P7-I1.
Notes: This section is part of Chapter II, 'Obligations of Data Fiduciary'.

Law Name: The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023
Verbatim Snippet: A Data Fiduciary shall make reasonable efforts to ensure the completeness, accuracy and consistency of the personal data, if the personal data is likely to be used to make a decision about the Data Principal or is to be disclosed to another Data Fiduciary.
Mapping Rationale: This section imposes an obligation on Data Fiduciaries to ensure the accuracy and completeness of personal data, contributing to the comprehensive data protection framework. Maps to P7-I1.
Notes: Part of the general obligations of Data Fiduciaries.

Law Name: The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023
Verbatim Snippet: A Data Fiduciary shall establish an effective mechanism to redress the grievances of Data Principals.
Mapping Rationale: This section mandates Data Fiduciaries to establish grievance redressal mechanisms for Data Principals, enhancing accountability and forming a key component of the comprehensive data protection framework. Maps to P7-I1.
Notes: This ensures a channel for Data Principals to address concerns regarding their data.

Law Name: The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023
Verbatim Snippet: A Data Fiduciary shall take reasonable security safeguards to prevent personal data breach.
Mapping Rationale: This section mandates Data Fiduciaries to implement reasonable security safeguards to prevent data breaches, indicating the presence of cybersecurity provisions within the data protection framework. Maps to P7-I2.
Notes: This provision addresses the security aspect of personal data processing.

Law Name: The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023
Verbatim Snippet: A Data Fiduciary shall erase personal data upon the Data Principal withdrawing her consent or as soon as it is reasonable to assume that the specified purpose would no longer be served, whichever is earlier, and the retention of personal data is no longer necessary for legal or business purposes.
Mapping Rationale: This section sets conditions for the erasure of personal data, thereby regulating data retention periods by requiring erasure when consent is withdrawn or purpose is served, unless necessary for legal/business purposes. Maps to P7-I3.
Notes: This provision defines the conditions under which personal data must be erased, impacting data retention policies.

Law Name: The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023
Verbatim Snippet: A Significant Data Fiduciary shall appoint a Data Protection Officer who shall be an individual based in India and be responsible to the Board for answering any communication made by the Data Principal for the purpose of exercising her rights under the provisions of this Act.
Mapping Rationale: This section mandates the appointment of a Data Protection Officer by Significant Data Fiduciaries, directly addressing DPO requirements. Maps to P7-I4.
Notes: The definition of 'Data Protection Officer' is in Section 2(l).

Law Name: The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023
Verbatim Snippet: for the fulfilment of any obligation under any law for the time being in force in India on the Data Fiduciary to disclose any information to the State or any instrumentality of the State
Mapping Rationale: This section permits processing of personal data without consent for fulfilling legal obligations to disclose information to the State or its instrumentalities, directly addressing government access requirements. Maps to P7-I5.
Notes: This is listed under 'certain legitimate uses' where consent is not required.

Law Name: Law of the Republic of Indonesia Number 27 of 2022 on Personal Data Protection

Verbatim Snippet: The processing of personal data by a data user shall be in compliance with the following Personal Data Protection Principles ... a data user who contravenes subsection (1) commits an offence

(2) It shall be the duty of a data user to take all reasonable steps to ensure that all personal data is destroyed or permanently deleted if it is no longer required

Verbatim Snippet: A data controller shall appoint one or more data protection officers who shall be accountable to the data controller for the compliance with this Act.

Verbatim Snippet: If an authorized officer is satisfied upon information received that he has reasonable cause to believe that by reason of delay in obtaining a search warrant under section 113 the investigation would be adversely affected ... the authorized officer may enter the premises
Mapping Rationale: This s. 114 authorises warrantless search/seizure in urgent cases. Maps to 7.5 (access without a court order).

Law Name: Personal Data Protection Act 2010, as amended by Act A1727
Verbatim Snippet: A data controller may transfer any personal data of a data subject to any place outside Malaysia if- (a) there is in that place in force any law which is substantially similar to this Act; or (b) that place ensures an adequate level of protection.
Mapping Rationale: The amended s. 129 replaced the former outright transfer prohibition with an adequacy/substantially-similar-law condition, making Malaysia's regime a conditional cross-border flow regime. Maps to P6-I4 (conditional flow regimes).
Notes: Same amended provision also cited at P6-I1 for the removal of the old ban; here it evidences the conditional-flow regime that replaced it.

Verbatim Snippet: An Act to enhance the national cyber security by providing for the establishment of the National Cyber Security Committee, the duties and powers of the Chief Executive of the National Cyber Security Agency, the functions and duties of the national critical information infrastructure sector leads and the national critical information infrastructure entities in implementing measures to manage the cyber security threats and cyber security incidents to national critical information infrastructures.
Mapping Rationale: The Cyber Security Act 2024 is Malaysia's first dedicated, horizontal cybersecurity statute (distinct from the narrower Computer Crimes Act 1997). Maps to P7-I2 (dedicated cybersecurity framework).
Notes: Source upgraded to the official AGC Laws-of-Malaysia PDF (superseding earlier non-official mirrors). Establishes NACSA, NCII sector leads, and licensing of cyber-security service providers.

Verbatim Snippet: A service provider shall not be held liable for infringement of copyright in any work where such infringement occurs by reason of the electronic copy of the work being stored at the direction of a user of its primary network, if the service provider does not have actual knowledge that the electronic copy of the work or the activity is infringing.
Mapping Rationale: This s. 43E establishes a knowledge-conditioned safe harbour excluding service-provider liability for user-directed storage of infringing works. Maps to P8-I1 as Malaysia's copyright-intermediary safe harbour (introduced by the 2012 amendment).
Notes: ss. 43C-43H form the full safe-harbour scheme (transmission, caching, storage, information-location tools) plus a notice-and-takedown mechanism. Verbatim confirmed against MyIPO official consolidated PDF.

Law Name: Personal Data Protection Act 2010, as amended by Act A1727
Verbatim Snippet: A data controller may transfer any personal data of a data subject to any place outside Malaysia if- (a) there is in that place in force any law which is substantially similar to this Act; or (b) that place ensures an adequate level of protection
Mapping Rationale: This amended s. 129(2) permits transfer subject to adequacy-style conditions. The old s.129(1) outright prohibition was deleted by Act A1727.
Notes: Version discrepancy flag - current law no longer contains the general ban; now fits 6.4 (conditional flow).

Verbatim Snippet: All records that relate to any business in Malaysia... shall be kept and retained in Malaysia.

Notes: Confirmed via Lexology plus the IRB's own official PDF. [Source updated: hasil.gov.my PDF unreachable; verbatim confirmed via official Laws of Malaysia (AGC).]

Verbatim Snippet: official secret means any document specified in the Schedule and any information and material relating thereto and includes any other official document, information and material as may be classified as 'Top Secret', 'Secret', 'Confidential' or 'Restricted'
Mapping Rationale: This s.2(1) allows any official document to be classified as an official secret at ministerial discretion. Maps to 2.2.
Notes: Verbatim confirmed against the official AGC PDF text. Highest-confidence row in this batch.

Verbatim Snippet: The purpose of this Subdivision is to restrict the remedies available against a NCP or NSP for rights infringements arising from their activities, but only if certain conditions are met.
Mapping Rationale: This s. 315 (with ss. 316-320) is Singapore's network-service-provider safe harbour, limiting the remedies available against a network content/service provider for copyright infringement where the statutory conditions are satisfied. Maps to P8-I1 as presence-of-safe-harbour evidence.
Notes: Section number is s.315 (Division 2, Part 8 of the Copyright Act 2021). s.320 confirms the conditions do not impose a general monitoring obligation. Verbatim confirmed against SSO official text.

Verbatim Snippet: A network service provider shall not be subject to any civil or criminal liability under any rule of law in respect of third-party material in the form of electronic records to which the network service provider merely provides access if such liability is founded on — (a) the making, publication, dissemination or distribution of such materials or any statement made in such material; or (b) the infringement of any rights subsisting in or in relation to such material.
Mapping Rationale: This s. 26(1) is Singapore's horizontal mere-conduit safe harbour, shielding network service providers from civil/criminal liability for third-party material they merely provide access to. Maps to P8-I2 (safe harbour for other illegal activities).
Notes: Applies only where the provider does no more than provide access; carve-outs apply to online-service-provider liability under Parts 12-13. Verbatim confirmed against the SSO official text. Verbatim confirmed against SSO 2020 Revised Edition PDF.

Verbatim Snippet: The accounting records and other records must be kept at such place or places within Singapore as the directors think fit.
Mapping Rationale: This subsection mandates that a company's accounting records and other related documents must be kept within Singapore, directly imposing a local storage requirement. Maps to P6-I2.
Notes: [UPDATED] The seed mentioned an amendment by the Corporate and Accounting Laws (Amendment) Act 2025. The retrieved text is from the current version of the Companies Act 1967 as at 17 Jul 2026 (Source [S1]), which incorporates this and any subsequent amendments, confirming the provision's continued relevance and wording.

Verbatim Snippet: must not transfer any personal data to a country or territory outside Singapore except in accordance with
Mapping Rationale: This s. 26(1) restricts overseas transfer unless comparable-protection requirements are met. Maps to 6.4 (conditional flow regime).
Notes: Version-currency flag: PDPA (Amendment) Regulations 2026 added Global CBPR and Global PRP.

Verbatim Snippet: An Act to govern the collection, use and disclosure of personal data by organisations, and to establish the Do Not Call Register and to provide for its administration, and for matters connected therewith.
Mapping Rationale: This Long Title establishes the overarching purpose of the Act, which is to govern the collection, use, and disclosure of personal data, thereby demonstrating the existence of a comprehensive legal framework for data protection. Maps to P7-I1 as evidence of such a framework.
Notes: [UPDATED] The Act has been amended multiple times since its enactment in 2012, with the latest amendment in force as of 01 Oct 2022. The seed entry is verified but the Act is updated.

Verbatim Snippet: The purpose of this Act is to safeguard individuals’ personal data against misuse, while supporting the legitimate needs of organisations to collect, use and disclose personal data for business and legal purposes.
Mapping Rationale: Section 3 explicitly states the Act's purpose to safeguard personal data, which is a foundational element of a comprehensive data protection framework. Maps to P7-I1 as evidence of such a framework.
Notes: [UPDATED] The Act has been amended multiple times since its enactment in 2012, with the latest amendment in force as of 01 Oct 2022. The seed entry is verified but the Act is updated.

Verbatim Snippet: An organisation must comply with the provisions of this Act when collecting, using or disclosing personal data.
Mapping Rationale: Section 11 imposes a general compliance obligation on organisations regarding personal data handling, indicating a core component of a comprehensive data protection framework. Maps to P7-I1 as evidence of such a framework.
Notes: [UPDATED] The Act has been amended multiple times since its enactment in 2012, with the latest amendment in force as of 01 Oct 2022. The seed entry is verified but the Act is updated.

Verbatim Snippet: An Act to require or authorise the taking of measures to prevent, manage and respond to cybersecurity threats and incidents, to regulate certain persons in relation to the cybersecurity of certain computers or computer systems, to regulate cybersecurity service providers, and for matters related thereto.
Mapping Rationale: This Long Title explicitly states the Act's purpose is to establish measures for preventing, managing, and responding to cybersecurity threats and incidents, and to regulate entities, thereby demonstrating a dedicated legal framework for cybersecurity.
Notes: [UPDATED] This provision is from the Cybersecurity Act 2018. The Act's text on the official source includes amendments from Act 19 of 2024, some of which (e.g., Parts 3A and 3B) come into force on 31 Oct 2025.

Verbatim Snippet: “cybersecurity” means the state in which a computer or computer system is protected from unauthorised access or attack, and because of that state — (a) the computer or computer system continues to be available and operational; (b) the integrity of the computer or computer system is maintained; and (c) the integrity and confidentiality of information stored in, processed by or transmitted through t
Mapping Rationale: This section provides a fundamental definition of “cybersecurity”, which is essential for any dedicated legal framework on the subject, establishing the scope and objectives of protection.
Notes: [UPDATED] This provision is from the Cybersecurity Act 2018. The Act's text on the official source includes amendments from Act 19 of 2024, some of which (e.g., Parts 3A and 3B) come into force on 31 Oct 2025.

Verbatim Snippet: 7.—(1) The Commissioner may, by notice in writing, designate a computer or computer system as a provider‑owned critical information infrastructure if the Commissioner is satisfied that — (a) the computer or computer system is necessary for the continuous delivery of an essential service; and (b) the loss or compromise of the computer or computer system will have a debilitating impact on the availa
Mapping Rationale: This section establishes the power to designate critical information infrastructure, a key component of a national cybersecurity framework for protecting essential services.
Notes: [UPDATED] This provision is from the Cybersecurity Act 2018. The Act's text on the official source includes amendments from Act 19 of 2024, some of which (e.g., Parts 3A and 3B) come into force on 31 Oct 2025.

Verbatim Snippet: 14.—(1) The owner of a provider‑owned critical information infrastructure must, as soon as reasonably practicable, notify the Commissioner of any cybersecurity incident in respect of the provider‑owned critical information infrastructure...
Mapping Rationale: This section imposes a duty to report cybersecurity incidents, which is a crucial operational requirement within a comprehensive cybersecurity legal framework.
Notes: [UPDATED] This provision is from the Cybersecurity Act 2018. The Act's text on the official source includes amendments from Act 19 of 2024, some of which (e.g., Parts 3A and 3B) come into force on 31 Oct 2025.

Verbatim Snippet: 16A.—(1) The Commissioner may, by notice in writing, designate a provider of an essential service as a designated provider responsible for the cybersecurity of a third‑party‑owned critical information infrastructure if the Commissioner is satisfied that the third‑party‑owned critical information infrastructure is necessary for the continuous delivery of an essential service...
Mapping Rationale: This new section extends the cybersecurity framework to cover third-party-owned critical information infrastructure, demonstrating an expansion and strengthening of the dedicated legal framework for cybersecurity.
Notes: [UPDATED] This section is part of Part 3A, introduced by Act 19 of 2024, and comes into force on 31 Oct 2025. The Act's text on the official source includes these future-dated amendments.

Verbatim Snippet: 16I.—(1) A designated provider must, as soon as reasonably practicable, notify the Commissioner of any cybersecurity incident in respect of the third‑party‑owned critical information infrastructure for which the designated provider is responsible...
Mapping Rationale: This new section imposes a duty to report cybersecurity incidents for third-party-owned CII, further expanding the operational requirements of the cybersecurity legal framework.

Mapping Rationale: This new section introduces the designation of "systems of temporary cybersecurity concern", indicating a flexible and adaptive expansion of the national cybersecurity legal framework to address emerging risks.

Verbatim Snippet: 17F.—(1) The owner of a system of temporary cybersecurity concern must, as soon as reasonably practicable, notify the Commissioner of any cybersecurity incident in respect of the system of temporary cybersecurity concern...
Mapping Rationale: This new section imposes a duty to report cybersecurity incidents for systems of temporary cybersecurity concern, further strengthening the incident response capabilities within the cybersecurity legal framework.
Notes: [UPDATED] This section is part of Part 3B, introduced by Act 19 of 2024, and comes into force on 31 Oct 2025. The Act's text on the official source includes these future-dated amendments.

Verbatim Snippet: 24.—(1) No person may provide any licensable cybersecurity service unless the person is a licensee.

Notes: [UPDATED] This section is part of Part 5, which came into operation on 11 April 2022. The Act's text on the official source includes amendments from Act 19 of 2024, some of which (e.g., Parts 3A and 3B) come into force on 31 Oct 2025.

Verbatim Snippet: An organisation must cease to retain documents containing personal data, or remove the means by which the personal data can be associated with particular individuals, as soon as it is reasonable to assume that — (a) the purpose for which that personal data was collected is no longer being served by retention of the personal data; and (b) retention is no longer necessary for legal or business purpo
Mapping Rationale: Section 25 mandates organisations to cease retaining personal data when its purpose is no longer served and retention is not necessary for legal/business purposes. This establishes a maximum retention principle, not a minimum retention period as implied by P7-I3.
Notes: [INVALID] The seed correctly identifies the law and section. However, the provision addresses the cessation of data retention (maximum retention principle) rather than establishing a minimum retention period, leading to an invalid mapping for P7-I3.

Mapping Rationale: This section mandates that organisations must designate one or more data protection officers, directly addressing the requirement for DPO under P7-I4.

Verbatim Snippet: The police officer or authorised person mentioned in subsection (1) is entitled to — (a) access any information, code or technology which has the capability of retransforming or unscrambling encrypted data into readable and comprehensible format or text for the purposes of investigating the arrestable offence; ... and (c) require any person whom the police officer or authorised person reasonably suspects to be in possession of any decryption information to grant access to such decryption information as may be necessary to decrypt any data required for the purpose of investigating the arrestable offence.
Mapping Rationale: This s. 40 empowers a police officer (on the Public Prosecutor's authorisation, not a court's) to compel decryption and technical assistance when investigating an arrestable offence. Maps to P7-I5 (government access to personal data).
Notes: Executive authorisation by the Public Prosecutor, not a court order. Verbatim per SSO official CPC 2010 text. Verbatim confirmed against SSO 2020 Revised Edition PDF.

Verbatim Snippet: Domestic and foreign enterprises ... that collect, analyse and process data concerning personal information, the relationship of service users, and data which is created by service users in Vietnam, must store this data in Vietnam for the period prescribed by the Government.
Mapping Rationale: This art. 26(3) imposes an in-country data-storage requirement on domestic and foreign providers of network/Internet services handling Vietnamese users' data. Maps to P6-I2 (local storage requirement).
Notes: No official English text exists; translation per widely-cited legal sources. Storage duration and covered entities detailed in implementing Decree 53/2022/ND-CP.

Verbatim Snippet: Additionally, foreign enterprises ... must set up representative offices or branches in Vietnam.
Mapping Rationale: This art. 26(3) requires in-scope foreign providers to establish a local branch or representative office in Vietnam. Maps to P6-I3 (infrastructure / local-presence requirement) as distinct from the data-storage limb.
Notes: Local-presence trigger and thresholds clarified by Decree 53/2022/ND-CP. Unofficial English translation.
